---
title: Self-host Anima
description: Run your own Anima instance. The backend is a stateless Go service and your notes stay on Walrus under your wallet, so the instance is disposable and the vault is not.
---

Running your own instance is the strongest form of "not hostage to any app."
The Anima backend holds no state: no database, no session store, no logged note
content. Your notes live on Walrus, sealed under your wallet's keys, not on the
server. So a self-hosted instance is a disposable front door to a vault you
already own. Kill it, move it, or run two of them, and the vault is untouched.
The same property is what lets a different client wake the same memory later
from the wallet alone.

This guide covers a testnet self-host. Read it end to end before you start,
because mainnet has an extra access dependency called out at the bottom.

## What you are running

The self-hostable stack is two pieces:

- **The static frontend.** A built single-page app you serve from any static
  host or CDN.
- **The backend.** A single stateless Go service (a chi server) that proxies
  chat and note-distillation to OpenRouter. It is the only thing you run as a
  long-lived process.

There is no separate chain service to operate. Chain interactions run
client-side in the app, signed by the user's wallet, so the writes to Walrus and
the reads from chain happen from the browser, not from a server you host. The
anima-mcp connector that lets your own agents read and write the vault also runs
user-side, alongside the agent, not as part of your instance. See the
[quickstart](/build/quickstart/) for how an agent pairs with a vault.

The Move contract is already deployed on chain. You do not redeploy it. You
point your instance at the existing on-chain package, the same one the hosted
app uses. Self-hosting changes who serves the app and proxies the LLM calls. It
does not fork the protocol.

:::note
Because the backend is stateless, it never sees a note in cleartext at rest and
stores nothing between requests. The only secrets it holds are its OpenRouter
key and its JWT signing secret, both supplied at runtime. That is the whole
custody argument: there is no server-side copy of your vault to lose, leak, or
hold hostage.
:::

## Before you start

You need:

- An OpenRouter API key (or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint, see
  [`OPENROUTER_BASE_URL`](#environment-reference)).
- A long, random string for `JWT_SECRET`. It signs the 24-hour login tokens
  your instance issues, so treat it like a password and generate it from a
  secure source.
- A host that can run a container and expose one HTTP port.
- The public origin where your frontend will be served (for example
  `https://notes.example.com`). You will need it for `ALLOWED_ORIGINS`.

:::caution
`ALLOWED_ORIGINS` is the setting most self-hosts get wrong. The backend rejects
browser requests from any origin not on this exact-match, comma-separated list.
If it is empty or does not include the origin your frontend is actually served
from, the app loads but every call to the backend fails on CORS. Set it to your
frontend's real origin before your first login attempt. For local development
that origin is typically `http://localhost:5173`.
:::

## Deploy with Coolify

The backend ships a `Dockerfile`, so Coolify can build and run it directly.

1. **Create the application.** In Coolify, add a new resource from your Git
   repository (or a registry image you have already built) and point it at the
   backend directory that contains the `Dockerfile`. Choose the Dockerfile build
   pack so Coolify builds from it rather than guessing a buildpack.
2. **Set the port.** The image listens on `8080`. Map your public port to
   container port `8080`. Let Coolify terminate TLS in front of it.
3. **Add the environment.** In the application's Environment Variables, set the
   values from the [reference](#environment-reference) below. At minimum:
   `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `JWT_SECRET`, and `ALLOWED_ORIGINS` (your frontend's
   public origin).
4. **Mark the secrets as secret.** Put `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` and `JWT_SECRET` in
   Coolify's secret store, not in a committed `.env`. Coolify injects them at
   runtime so they never land in your repository or the build image.
5. **Deploy.** Coolify builds the image and runs the container. The service is
   stateless, so no volumes and no database to provision.

Serve the static frontend separately (Coolify can host a static site, or use any
CDN). Build the frontend pointed at your backend's public URL and the testnet
package, then make sure your backend's `ALLOWED_ORIGINS` lists the exact origin
the frontend is served from.

## Deploy with plain Docker

If you are not using Coolify, the same `Dockerfile` runs anywhere Docker does.

Build the image from the backend directory:

```sh
docker build -t anima-backend .
```

Run it, injecting secrets and config as runtime environment, not baked into the
image:

```sh
docker run -p 8080:8080 \
  -e OPENROUTER_API_KEY="$OPENROUTER_API_KEY" \
  -e JWT_SECRET="$JWT_SECRET" \
  -e ALLOWED_ORIGINS="https://notes.example.com" \
  anima-backend
```

Reading the secret values from your shell environment (the `"$VAR"` form above)
keeps them out of your command history and out of any file. For a longer-lived
setup, use Docker secrets or your orchestrator's secret mechanism and pass the
values in at run time. Put the container behind a reverse proxy that terminates
TLS, the same way Coolify does it for you.

To confirm the service is up, request a nonce:

```sh
curl https://notes.example.com/auth/nonce
# {"nonce":"anima:1749600000000:9f2c..."}
```

A nonce response means the backend is running and reachable. Login itself is
done by the app from the user's wallet.

## Keep secrets out of the repo

Two values are sensitive: `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` and `JWT_SECRET`. Both are
supplied at runtime and never need to live in source.

- **Never commit a `.env` with real secrets.** If you keep a `.env` for local
  development, keep it git-ignored and fill it with throwaway values.
- **In Coolify,** store both in the secret store so they are injected at runtime
  and excluded from the build image and logs.
- **In plain Docker,** prefer Docker secrets or runtime `-e` injection from your
  shell or orchestrator over an env file checked into the repo.
- **Rotate `JWT_SECRET` to log everyone out.** Changing it invalidates every
  outstanding 24-hour token, which is the lever you reach for if a token leaks.

The instance never logs request bodies, query strings, `Authorization` headers,
or message content on any path, including errors. The request log records method,
path, status, and latency, and nothing else. That is a deliberate custody
invariant, so your self-host does not accumulate a shadow copy of note content in
its logs.

## Environment reference

| Variable | Required | Default | Notes |
|----------|----------|---------|-------|
| `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` | yes | none | Key for OpenRouter (or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint). |
| `JWT_SECRET` | yes | none | Signs 24-hour login tokens. Use a long random value. Rotate to invalidate all tokens. |
| `ALLOWED_ORIGINS` | for browser use | empty (no browser origins allowed) | Comma-separated, exact-match list of frontend origins. Set to your frontend's public origin. |
| `PORT` | no | `8080` | Port the service listens on. |
| `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL` | no | `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` | Point at any OpenAI-compatible API, including your own inference endpoint. |
| `RATE_LIMIT_PER_MIN` | no | `30` | Per-login rate limit for chat and distill. |
| `DEFAULT_MODEL` | no | `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5` | A request can override the model per call. |

## What a self-host gives you today

The app frontend is being rebuilt, so a self-hosted instance today gives you the
capability-level UI rather than the finished product surface. The part that is
durable now, and the reason to self-host at all, is the path underneath the UI:

- Your wallet owns the vault.
- Your notes are sealed to Walrus under your keys.
- Your own agents read and write that vault through anima-mcp, running on your
  side, paired to your vault. See the [quickstart](/build/quickstart/) and the
  [FAQ](/build/faq/) for the agent path.

That path does not depend on which copy of the app you run, which is the whole
point. Self-hosting is one more client in front of a vault you already own, not
a place your data goes to live.

## Mainnet is not turnkey yet

Scope your self-host to testnet for now. The end-to-end seal-to-storage flow on
mainnet depends on Seal mainnet access, which is permissioned (for example via
Enoki) rather than open. Until that access is in hand, a self-hosted mainnet
instance is not a flip-a-switch operation, even though the contract is deployed.

:::caution
Run your self-host against testnet. Mainnet self-hosting is gated on Seal mainnet
access and is not turnkey today. The testnet path above is the supported one.
:::
